ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general grouper entry (FOODON_03411433) but no specific entry for the Dragon Tiger Grouper hybrid (Epinephelus fuscoguttatus × E. lanceolatus). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers Epinephelus genus groupers. Dragon Tiger Grouper is a hybrid of E. fuscoguttatus × E. lanceolatus; no species-specific hybrid code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Dragon Tiger Grouper or hybrid grouper species. SNOMED CT does not maintain detailed fish taxonomy at hybrid level.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Dragon Tiger Grouper is approximately 150 g of cooked fish flesh, often presented whole or as a fillet portion with condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Grouper, raw (Epinephelus spp.) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 15033
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Dragon Tiger Grouper (Epinephelus fuscoguttatus × E. lanceolatus hybrid) is not individually listed in any approved primary regional database. USDA SR Legacy grouper (Epinephelus spp., raw) is used as the closest defensible analogue. Values represent raw fish per 100 g. Cooking method (steaming) would slightly concentrate protein and reduce water content; values are presented as raw-equivalent per 100 g consistent with source. Sodium value reflects raw fish; restaurant preparations with soy sauce will substantially increase sodium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dragon Tiger Grouper. Pure protein-dominant fish with negligible carbohydrate content (0 g per 100 g) has an effectively negligible glycemic response. GI is estimated at 5 (analogous to other lean fish such as cod and grouper) based on the principle that foods with near-zero carbohydrate content have very low GI values, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for lean fish. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (5 × 0.0 × 150/100) / 100 = 0.0.

hypertension.guidance: The raw fish itself is low in sodium (53 mg per 100 g), but restaurant preparations of Dragon Tiger Grouper typically involve soy sauce, oyster sauce, or salted condiments that can raise the sodium content of a serving to 600–900 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparations or consume this dish in moderation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high lean protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dragon Tiger Grouper contains virtually no carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g) and has a glycemic index of approximately 5, making it an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; it supports satiety and glycemic control when prepared without sugar-based sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high lean protein, and omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dragon Tiger Grouper is a lean fish with low saturated fat (0.2 g per 100 g) and a favourable polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio; regular consumption of lean fish is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk, though sodium from restaurant sauces should be monitored in individuals with existing cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dragon Tiger Grouper (龙虎斑, Lóng Hǔ Bān) is a premium hybrid marine fish produced by crossing the Brown-marbled Grouper (Epinephelus fuscoguttatus) with the Giant Grouper (Epinephelus lanceolatus). It is widely farmed and consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, and southern China, and is a prized seafood in Chinese cuisine for its firm, white, mildly sweet flesh and minimal pin bones. in Singapore, it is most commonly prepared by steaming whole or as a fillet with light soy sauce, ginger, spring onion, and hot sesame oil — a preparation that preserves its delicate flavour. It may also be served deep-fried, braised in soy sauce, or in hot pot. Nutritionally, Dragon Tiger Grouper is a high-protein, low-fat fish providing approximately 19.4 g protein and only 1.0 g fat per 100 g, with an energy density of 92 kcal per 100 g. It is rich in selenium, potassium, phosphorus, and B vitamins including B12 and niacin. Its carbohydrate content is negligible (0 g), giving it a very low glycemic index of approximately 5, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-carbohydrate diets. The raw fish is low in sodium, but restaurant preparations with soy-based sauces can significantly increase sodium intake, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Dragon Tiger Grouper is halal-certified in most Singapore contexts and is dairy-free and gluten-free in its natural form. It is a culturally significant dish often served at Chinese banquets, family celebrations, and seafood restaurants along Singapore's waterfront.

confidence_reason: Dragon Tiger Grouper as a specific hybrid species is not individually listed in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy grouper (Epinephelus spp.) as the closest approved analogue. GI is estimated from analogous lean fish data in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the biological similarity between Dragon Tiger Grouper and other Epinephelus groupers is high, and the macronutrient profile is well-supported by the analogue source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy grouper (Epinephelus spp., raw) as no approved primary source lists Dragon Tiger Grouper specifically. Values represent the raw fish; steaming will slightly concentrate protein and reduce water content. 2. Carbohydrate_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw grouper; grouper is a pure protein-and-fat food with no glycemic carbohydrates. Sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; fish contains no dietary fibre. 3. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw grouper. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw grouper. 4. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy grouper entry used as the analogue source. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy grouper entry used as the analogue source. 5. Sodium value (53 mg per 100 g) reflects raw fish only; restaurant preparations with soy sauce, oyster sauce, or salted condiments will substantially increase sodium per serving. 6. GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous lean fish data; no direct GI measurement for Dragon Tiger Grouper was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 7. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON included 'Crab' and 'Lobster' which are not relevant to this dish; these have been disregarded as field survey platform tagging artefacts. 8. Halal status is assumed based on the fish being a permissible species under Islamic dietary law; kosher status is not confirmed. 9. Follow-up recommended: seek HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for hybrid grouper if published in future.
